title: The thumbs-down spike
prompt: You're a PM on Quill, an AI writing assistant that drafts emails, blog posts, and code comments. Over the last week, the thumbs-down rate on generated drafts jumped from about 4% to about 9%. Leadership wants to know what's going on and what you'd do about it. Walk me through how you'd approach it.

hidden_facts.onset: The spike started 3 days ago, not gradually — it was a step change.
hidden_facts.segment_usecase: It's concentrated in the 'email' use case. Blog and code drafts are unchanged.
hidden_facts.segment_cohort: Thumbs-down for users on model version v4.2 is ~14%; users still on v4.1 are ~4%.
hidden_facts.release: A new model version, v4.2, shipped 3 days ago to 50% of users as an A/B test.
hidden_facts.instrumentation: No logging or metric-definition changes were made in this period.
hidden_facts.latency: p95 latency on v4.2 is ~400ms higher than v4.1.
hidden_facts.volume: Overall usage volume is flat; no traffic-mix change.
hidden_facts.pricing_ui: No pricing or UI changes shipped in this window.
hidden_facts.external: No known competitor launch or seasonality effect.

ideal_answer_notes: Define the metric precisely first: what counts as a thumbs-down, per draft vs per session, and over what denominator.
Characterize the pattern before hypothesizing: sudden step change (points at a release/experiment, not seasonality), and check whether it's segment-wide or isolated.
Segment aggressively — by use case (email vs blog vs code), by model arm (v4.1 vs v4.2), by platform/geo. The email + v4.2 concentration is the key signal.
Split causes MECE into internal (a release/experiment like v4.2, instrumentation bug, metric-definition change, pricing/UX change) vs external (seasonality, competitor, macro).
Use process of elimination, not a question dump: instrumentation unchanged, volume flat, no UI/pricing change -> internal release is the leading suspect.
Confirm via the A/B arm comparison (14% vs 4%) rather than declaring the cause — this is the experiment already running.

AI-specific: treat thumbs-down as a quality/guardrail metric; consider an eval-set regression, a tone/hallucination change in v4.2, and the quality-vs-latency tradeoff (v4.2 is slower AND worse here).
Short-term mitigation: roll back or hold v4.2 for the email use case; add thumbs-down and latency as launch-gate guardrail metrics.
Longer term: add a pre-launch eval suite segmented by use case so a regression like this is caught before a 50% rollout.
extra_checklist: Isolates the regression to the v4.2 model arm using the running A/B rather than guessing
Frames thumbs-down as a model-quality guardrail and asks about recent model/version changes early
Recognizes v4.2 is worse on BOTH quality and latency (a real model tradeoff, not just a bug)

anchors.strong: Defines the metric, immediately asks about recent releases and segments by use case and model arm, isolates it to email on v4.2 via the running experiment, proposes a rollback + guardrail metrics, and calls out the missing pre-launch eval suite — all as a structured elimination, not a guess.
anchors.at_bar: Structures internal-vs-external causes and gets to 'a recent model change' with a couple of nudges, segments partially, and proposes a reasonable next step, but doesn't fully exploit the A/B arm or the eval/latency angle without prompting.
anchors.weak: Jumps straight to hypotheses ('maybe the prompt is bad', 'users are pickier') without defining the metric, doesn't segment, dumps a list of possible causes without a way to confirm any, and never asks whether anything shipped.
